dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 185 for a 10-year-old male with possible seizure at 5234 Vine Street in the city of Richmond.
Hi ma'am, I'm Medic Clapp with the rescue squad. My partner is Medic Rice. What made you call 911 for your grandson today?
He had a seizure in his room. My husband and I tried to help him.
Did the seizure last longer than five minutes, or did he have another seizure right after?
It wasn't long, but he was unresponsive afterward and his breathing was shallow.
We saw he was already on the couch and you were doing chest compressions. That's great under pressure.
Can your grandson open his eyes right now?
No, he's very still. I squeezed his shoulder, and he didn't react.
He may be post-seizure. Medics Clapp, let's proceed with GCS evaluation.
He's not opening his eyes to verbal cues. Would you consider a gentle tap or rubbing his sternum? This will help determine eye response to pain.
Yes, we can try that.
Alright, let's go with that.
His eyes opened slowly but only after the pain stimulus. Great response to avoid violating protocol.
So, he opened his eyes to pain. That's a GCS of 2 for eye response.
Does he make any sounds or try to speak right now?
No verbal response. He just lies there.
That's a GCS of 1 for verbal. Not surprising for a post-seizure state.
When we rubbed his sternum, he moved his arms slightly.
That's a motor response of 1, I think. Let me double-check the GCS guide.
Let's check his airway. Can we move his head a bit to test for patent airway?
Go ahead.
Airway appears patent. He's not coughing nor making stridulous sounds. This looks clear.
Let's take your child's oxygen saturation. It might be low due to generalized tonic-clonic movements.
Let me apply the pulse oximeter now.
The reading is 92%.
His oxygen is dropping. We'll apply nasal cannula at 2L. Alright, you both had hands on him-were his breaths really shallow during the seizure?
Yes, he was gasping at times.
His breathing rate is about 20 breaths per minute.
Using nasal cannula at 2L.
Now we're at 98%. That's a positive response.
Thank you for handling him. His breathing is still shallow-about 20 breaths a minute.
Are there any adventitious sounds in either lung?
Clear air movement. No added sounds.
His radial pulse is strong.
Pupils are equal and reactive. That is PEARL.
Now I'll take the blood pressure.
Blood pressure is 127/83 and his pulse is 84.
Good. His skin is pink and warm with normal turgor.
Yes, consistent with a postictal state. Let's be ready for possible further intervention.
You mentioned your grandson had a seizure. How often has he had them in the past?
He was on Keppra for about two years ago. It was kept in control.
Is he currently taking Keppra for his seizures?
Yes. And he had a mild concussion before, which the doctors call traumatic brain injury.
Okay, so he had one episode today and hadn't had one in over a year. According to you, the medication was continued through 21:04?
Yes, it was last taken right at 8:30.

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. - events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: Any injuries from this episode?

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. - events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: No, but he vomited once before the seizure began.

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. - events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: And later again when you brought him out. Okay.
We'll take his blood glucose now. Sorry, but this will sting a little.
Blood glucose is 149. That's normal.
I'm going to check his eyes. His pupils are equal and reactive. There is no jugular venous distension.
Any pain or swelling on the head or behind the neck?
No sign of trauma.
I'll put him on cardiac monitoring now.
At this time, it shows a normal sinus rhythm.
CV-EKG with 4 lead monitoring is now active.
Let me initiate an IV now. I'll use the left arm.
IV has been placed on the left arm. Just ensuring it's patent with some saline.
We just placed the IV and have applied cardiac rhythm monitoring, as per medical-seizure protocol 9-12. That's consistent with his GCS and past medical history.
Also consistent with his currently altered mental status and full-length IV placement.
Taking vitals again now that the IV is active.
Pulse is down to 72.
Blood pressure is now 124/86.
Oxygen remains good at 95%.
Blood glucose remains stable at 149.
His vitals stabilized, and he is still postictal.
Oxygen saturation peaked at 96% before transport.
There is normal cardiorespiratory function currently without signs of deterioration.
I've placed an IV into his left arm. Just ensuring it's patent with some saline.
IV is flushed and working well. I'll finish filling the emergency bag before transport.
On a scale from 0 to 10, with ten being the worst, how would you rate his pain?
He actually shows no sign of pain. He just stares blankly now.
So the pain is most likely a 0 in this case.
He seems to be in a postictal state as he's unresponsive but shows some motor response to pain.
Any recent illness or injury around this time?
No, he hasn't been sick recently.
Would the family like transport to VCU?
Yes. That's our usual hospital.
Father, we're going to fasten the seatbelts to both the patient and you.
He's seated upright on the stretcher with armrests in. That should hold.
I have secured the emergency bag and am ready.
Patient is being transported cold as per protocol.
The pediatric team is waiting at VCU ED. Emergency physician-Dr. Dunbar.
Thanks for riding with us, sir. We handed him off to clinical staff.
Scene time was a total of less than 10 minutes; left at 21:10.
His SpO2 has improved to 95%.
Blood glucose remains at 149.
No jugular venous distension observed currently.
EKG remains in sinus rhythm with no arrhythmias.
He has no signs of trauma from the episode, but we've documented the two vomiting episodes.
Normal SpO2 reading on board oxygen of 95%.
Respirations are 20 per minute and equal bilaterally, with no signs of respiratory distress.
Blood pressure is still 124/86. Pupils remain equal and reactive.
Pulse is strong and regular at 72.
Skin is pink, warm, and dry with no rashes, bruising, or abrasions.

dialogue.role: nose, mouth and throat exam; Partner
dialogue.utterance: No obstruction in the mouth or nose. No drainage. Ears appear clear.

dialogue.role: nose, mouth and throat exam; Medic
dialogue.utterance: He wasn't swallowing anything other than saliva. All structures are intact.
Oxygen rates remain stable with nasal cannula at 2L.
Normal glucose levels have remained steady at 149.
Heartbeat remains steady on monitor. No changes in rhythm.
No medications administered other than supportive interventions like IV and oxygen.
No use of anti-convulsants due to normal vital signs.
Pulse still at 72 with no signs of arrhythmia.
Temporal changes in blood pressure and oxygen levels remain within safe margins for transport.
No new interventions required; IV remains patent and secure.
VCU is the nearest pediatric trauma center and appropriate for our patient.
All seatbelts and restraints secured during in-transit loading.
